overview: (1) The Gemara discusses the laws of a disputed document found in the market, analyzing the opinions of רבי, רבי שמעון בן גמליאל, and רבי יוסי regarding its validation and return. (2) The Gemara discusses the division of a document between the main body (טופס) and the remainder (תורף), and whether they are divided or taken separately. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Gemara discusses the laws of a disputed document found in the market, analyzing the opinions of רבי, רבי שמעון בן גמליאל, and רבי יוסי regarding its validation and return.
The Gemara discusses the division of a document between the main body (טופס) and the remainder (תורף), and whether they are divided or taken separately. — discussion continues into next amud.

steps.title: רבא's Explanation: Fear of Repayment
steps.stepSummary: Rava explains that the baraita teaches that even a validated document should not be returned to the creditor because we suspect the debt was already repaid (חיישינן לפירעון).
steps.whatsHappening: רבא provides the rationale for the baraita's ruling. Even if the document is validated, it should not be returned to the creditor.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא explains that the baraita teaches a stringency: even a validated document is not returned. The reason is חיישינן לפירעון: we suspect the debtor repaid the debt and lost the document. This presumption of repayment overrides the document's validity.
steps.whatToRemember: The court withholds the document due to suspicion of repayment.
steps.confusionAlert: This seems to contradict רבי יוסי's view that the document retains its presumptive status.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes the principle of חַיְּשִׁינַן לְפִרְעוֹן as a major factor in document disputes.

phrases.aramaic: וְלָא מִיבַּעְיָא לָא כְּתַב בֵּיהּ הֶנְפֵּק,
phrases.english: And it is not necessary to say that it is not written in it 'he…

phrases.aramaic: דְּאִיכָּא לְמֵימַר כָּתַב לִלְוֹת וְלֹא לָוָה,
phrases.english: for there is to say he wrote to borrow and did not borrow,
but even if it is written in it 'he paid out',
for it is established – he will not return, for we are concerned for repayment.

steps.triggerLanguage: ולא מיבעיא לא כתב ביה הנפק... אלא אפילו כתב ביה הנפק, דמקוים – לא יחזיר, דחיישינן לפירעון

rashi.hebrew: לפירעון - שמא אמת טוען הלוה שפרעו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the concern of חיישינן לפירעון means we suspect the debtor's claim that he already repaid the debt is true.

steps.title: Challenge to רבי יוסי's Consistency
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges רבי יוסי's position by citing a baraita about a found marriage contract (שטר כתובה) where רבי יוסי rules that if the woman is widowed or divorced, the document should not be returned, implying he does suspect repayment.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a difficulty against רבי יוסי's statement that the document retains its presumptive status. This is a classic קושיא.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא cites a baraita about a found שטר כתובה. רבי יוסי rules that if the woman is widowed or divorced, the document is not returned. This implies he suspects repayment when the debt is due. This contradicts his earlier ruling that the document retains its presumptive status.
steps.whatToRemember: רבי יוסי seems to suspect repayment in the case of a שטר כתובה.
steps.confusionAlert: The challenge hinges on whether רבי יוסי consistently applies חַיְּשִׁינַן לְפִרְעוֹן.
steps.whyThisMatters: This forces the גמרא to reconcile רבי יוסי's seemingly contradictory positions.

phrases.english: And Rabbi Yosei says:

phrases.aramaic: הֲרֵי הוּא בְּחֶזְקָתוֹ וְלָא חָיְישִׁינַן לְפֵירָעוֹן.
phrases.english: Behold, it is in its presumptive status, and we do not suspect repayment.
And does Rabbi Yosei not suspect repayment?
And isn't it taught: One who found a marriage contract in the marketplace,
When the husband admits — he returns to the woman,
The husband does not admit —

phrases.aramaic: לֹא יַחֲזִיר לֹא לָזֶה וְלֹא לָזֶה.
phrases.english: He does not return neither to this one nor to that one.
Rabbi Yosei says:
She is still under her husband — he returns to the woman.
She was widowed or divorced —

phrases.aramaic: לֹא יַחֲזִיר לֹא לָזֶה וְלֹא לָזֶה.
phrases.english: He does not return neither to this one nor to that one.

steps.triggerLanguage: ורבי יוסי אמר: הרי הוא בחזקתו ולא חיישינן לפירעון. ולא חייש רבי יוסי לפירעון?

rashi.hebrew: ורבי יוסי לא חייש לפירעון - דמאן דמהדרי ליה שטר פרוע לאלתר זהיר ביה לקורעו ולא משהי ליה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבי יוסי does not suspect repayment (פירעון) because a person who receives a paid document tears it up immediately rather than keeping it.

rashi.hebrew: שהבעל מודה - שלא נתן לה כתובתה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that 'the husband admits' means he admits he has not yet paid the כתובה.

rashi.hebrew: אין הבעל מודה - שאמר פרעתי ומידי נפל:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that 'the husband does not admit' means he claims he already paid and the document fell out of his possession.
רש״י explains that 'she is still under her husband' means she has not been divorced.

rashi.hebrew: יחזיר לאשה - דלא עביד איניש ליתן כתובה קודם גירושין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the document is returned to the wife because a person does not typically pay the כתובה before divorce.

tosafot.hebrew: בזמן שהבעל מודה יחזיר לאשה - דלא חיישינן לקנוניא וליכא למיחש לשמא כתב בניסן ולא נשא עד תשרי דמנה ומאתים משתעבדי מן האירוסין אף בלא כתובה דאירוסין קלא אית להו ולקודם אירוסין ליכא למיחש דכתב לה ותוספת נמי לאביי דאמר עדיו בחתומיו זכין לו א"ש ור' אסי יעמיד כשהקנה לה:
tosafot.english: If the husband concedes [that he has not yet paid the Kesubah, the finder] should return [the Kesubah document] to the wife. Why is it necessary for the Baraisa to teach us that when the husband admits that he has not yet paid the Kesubah, the Kesubah document is returned to his wife? It seems unnecessary, since he is telling us he is still obligated to pay the Kesubah. Tosfos will explain the possible concerns that we may have about the validity of the document even when the husband admits that he still owes the Kesubah. We must keep in mind that we should be very concerned about the loss of a document. It raises questions in our minds.1See Rashi (12b) ד'ה הנה ריעי. If in fact it was a valid document, how did it happen that the wife lost it? Most people are extremely careful about caring for their documents, especially those that represent a considerable sum. The loss causes us to think of possible reasons for the careless treatment of the document. Tosfos will examine each possible reason that would render the document invalid and thus explain why the wife was not as careful with it as she should have been. a) קנוניא - conspiracy. There is a possibility that the husband already paid the Kesubah. After paying the Kesubah, the husband then conspired with his wife and returned the document to her so that she would attempt to collect with the document a second time. The husband would then claim that he has no money with which to pay and she would then proceed to collect from properties that he sold. After collecting from the purchasers of his sold properties, the couple would then divide the illegally collected funds. b) שמא כתב בניסן ולא נשא עד תשרי - perhaps the document was written in Nissan with the intention of marrying then. However, the wedding did not take place until the following Tishrei. According to the document, any property sold between Nissan and Tishrei is collateral for the Kesubah, but actually the marriage took place the following Tishrei and those properties may not be subject to the lien. c) קודם אירוסין - Perhaps the document was written even before the betrothal. The lien on properties sold by the husband is in effect only for such property he owned at the time of the betrothal. According to the written document even such properties sold before the betrothal are illegally subject to the lien of the Kesubah. d) תוספות - the voluntary addition to the Kesubah by the husband may differ from the initial Kesubah instituted by the Rabanan. Tosfos explains: By teaching us that when the husband admits that he did not yet pay the Kesubah, the Kesubah document is returned to the wife, the Baraisa is informing us that: We are not concerned about a) conspiracy between husband and wife to defraud the purchasers of his sold properties. There is also no concern that b) perhaps he wrote the Kesubah in Nissan but did not marry until the following Tishrei, which would render the properties sold between Nissan and Tishrei illegally subject to the Kesubah lien, for the one hundred zuz of a widow’s Kesubah and the two hundred zuz of a virgin's Kesubah are actually mortgaged to the lien from the time of betrothal, even when a Kesubah document was not written, because betrothal generates publicity and the purchasers of the husband’s property are aware of the Chassan’s potential liability for the Kesubah payment even though the wedding did not take place for many months afterward. Since they are aware of his liability, the lien on his property sold between Nissan and the following Tishrei is legal and they are obligated to pay when the husband has no funds with which to pay the Kesubah. But what about possibility c) that the Kesubah may have been written even before betrothal? There is no concern that the Kesubah document may have been written before betrothal, which would then establish an illegal lien on property that the husband sold before betrothal. We have explained that the lien of a Kesubah is accomplished by the betrothal even though a Kesubah document was not written. The reason for this is quite simple. As Tosfos explained earlier, betrothal is public information. Whenever one betroths his Kallah, everyone knows that the Chassan now has a Kesubah obligation. Thus they know to be careful when purchasing property from him. When they do purchase from him they are fully aware of the possible Kesubah liability and they have chosen that it is worth their while to take the risk. This logic is quite applicable to the amount that the Chassan is required to give his Kallah by Rabbinic edict. Everyone is aware that the Chassan has an obligation to pay the one hundred or two hundred zuz the Rabanan mandated. However, very often (in today’s Kesubos this is universal) the Chassan voluntarily adds to the amount obligated by the Rabanan. This additional amount is not common knowledge, for not everybody knows if and how much the Chassan voluntarily added to the Kesubah. Thus, the lien automatically established by betrothal is effective only insofar as the required one or two hundred zuz is concerned. There is no automatic lien as far as the additional amount is concerned. If so, we are now faced with a problem: Tosfos explained earlier that we are not concerned about whether the Kesubah may have been written in Nissan and the actual wedding took place in Tishrei, for in any case the lien of Kesubah is established at the time of betrothal. But this is only true of the mandated amount, not for the additional voluntary sum. We should then be concerned that perhaps the Kesubah was written in Nissan and they first married the following Tishrei and the lien on properties sold before Tishrei is illegal? The Gemara later (12b) discusses a similar problem. But that which we learned in a Mishna: We may write a loan document for the borrower even though the lender is not present. We assume that the borrower who stands to lose if the document reaches the lender before the loan occurs will not allow this to happen. The Gemara asks: Why are we initially allowed to write [this loan document]? We should be concerned that [the borrower] wrote the document with the intention of borrowing in Nissan, but the actual loan may not happen till later in Tishrei, and [the lender] will come to collect from the purchasers of property sold between Nissan and Tishrei illegally? Since the loan did not happen till Tishrei, property sold by the lender before Tishrei should not be subject to the lien. However, the document does include those properties in the lien. This can lead to an illegal collection by the lender from the purchaser of these properties. Rav Assi said: We are speaking of documents of acquisition where the borrower specifically states that he is giving the lender a lien as of the date of the document even if there will never be a loan. This is effective in establishing a lien, for [the borrower] has accepted this obligation upon himself. Abaye disagrees: Abaye says: the witnesses of the document, by signing it, establish a lien for the benefit [of the lender]. Even if it is not a document of acquisition. Abaye maintains that the very act of witnesses signing a loan document establishes a lien on the borrower’s property even if the loan never took place. Thus according to Abaye as long as a Kesubah document is signed, a lien is established even for the voluntary additional amount added to the Kesubah by the Chassan. According to Abaye, who says that when witnesses sign a document they establish a lien for the benefit of the [lender], it comes out well that even insofar as the additional amount as well, that we need not be concerned there may be an illegal lien established by the Kesubah if it was written in Nissan and the wedding did not take place till Tishrei, because the effective date of the lien is the date it was signed, not the date when the loan or the wedding took place. Rav Assi who disagrees with Abaye and does not hold that the signing of the document automatically establishes a lien on the [property of the borrower will interpret our Baraisa as speaking of when the husband specifically wrote the Kesubah as a document of acquisition. It is only then that we would not be concerned that there is an illegal lien established for the additional amount by the document.
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות questions why the baraita needs to teach that the document is returned when the husband admits, and explains that we must consider concerns like קנוניא (conspiracy) or the document being written before the marriage (ניסן) but the marriage occurring later (תשרי), which could affect the validity of the debt.

steps.title: Resolution by Reversing the Tannaim
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the difficulty by suggesting that the order of the Tannaim in the first baraita should be reversed: רבי יוסי holds the document retains its status, and the Rabbis suspect repayment.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא proposes a textual emendation to resolve the contradiction in רבי יוסי's rulings. This is a standard תירוץ.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא suggests reversing the opinions in the first baraita. רבי יוסי would then say לא יוציאו עולמית, suspecting repayment. This would align his view in both baraitot: he consistently suspects repayment when the debt is due.
steps.whatToRemember: The גמרא tries to fix the contradiction by swapping the opinions.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a common Talmudic technique to resolve contradictions between baraitot.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the next challenge: if we swap the opinions, we create a new contradiction for the Rabbis.

phrases.aramaic: אֵיפוֹךְ: נָפַל לְיַד דַּיָּין לֹא יוֹצִיאוֹ עוֹלָמִית,
phrases.english: Reverse: If it fell into the hand of a judge, it shall not be removed forever,
words of Rabbi Yosei.
And the Sages say: Behold, it is in its presumptive status.

steps.triggerLanguage: איפוך: נפל ליד דיין לא יוציאו עולמית, דברי רבי יוסי

steps.title: New Challenge: Contradiction Within the Rabbis
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara points out that reversing the Tannaim creates a new contradiction: the Rabbis would now suspect repayment in the promissory note case but not in the marriage contract case.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a difficulty against the proposed resolution. This is a follow-up קושיא.
steps.deeperAnalysis: If we reverse the opinions, the Rabbis in the first baraita would hold no suspicion of repayment. But in the second baraita, they rule that if the husband does not admit, the document is not returned. This creates a contradiction within the Rabbis' own rulings.

keyTerms.meaning: A contradiction within the Rabbis' rulings

steps.whatToRemember: Reversing the Tannaim creates a new problem for the Rabbis.
steps.confusionAlert: The גמרא is showing that the simple reversal doesn't work.
steps.whyThisMatters: This forces the גמרא to find a more nuanced resolution.

phrases.english: If so, the Rabbis' statement is difficult against the Rabbis' statement!

steps.title: Resolution: The Marriage Contract Baraita is Entirely רבי יוסי
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the difficulty by stating that the baraita about the marriage contract is entirely according to רבי יוסי, and the anonymous ruling is incomplete, referring only to cases where the woman is widowed or divorced.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא provides a resolution to the contradiction within the Rabbis' rulings. This is the final תירוץ for this section.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא explains that the baraita about the שטר כתובה is entirely רבי יוסי's opinion. The anonymous ruling is incomplete and should be understood as applying only to cases where the woman is widowed or divorced. This aligns with רבי יוסי's explicit statement, preserving consistency.

keyTerms.meaning: The entire baraita is רבי יוסי's opinion

steps.whatToRemember: The marriage contract baraita is entirely רבי יוסי's opinion.
steps.confusionAlert: This resolution relies on the assumption that the anonymous ruling is incomplete.
steps.whyThisMatters: This preserves the consistency of רבי יוסי's view without reversing the Tannaim.

phrases.english: A ketubah document, entirely Rabbi Yosei,
And it is lacking a missing part and thus it teaches:
phrases.notes: The text is missing words, so the Gemara reconstructs the missing context.

phrases.english: The husband does not admit –

phrases.aramaic: לֹא יַחֲזִיר לֹא לָזֶה וְלֹא לָזֶה.
phrases.english: He shall not return neither to this one nor to that one.
In what matters are these words said –
That she was widowed or that she was divorced,
But she is still under her husband –
He shall return to the woman, that Rabbi Yosei says:
She is still under her husband – he shall return to the woman,
She was widowed or that she was divorced –

phrases.aramaic: לֹא יַחֲזִיר לֹא לָזֶה וְלֹא לָזֶה.
phrases.english: He shall not return neither to this one nor to that one.

steps.triggerLanguage: שטר כתובה, כולה רבי יוסי, וחסורי מחסרא והכי קתני

steps.title: רַב פַּפָּא's Resolution: רַבִּי יוֹסֵה Argues Within the רַבָּנִים's Framework
steps.stepSummary: Rav Pappa offers an alternative resolution: רבי יוסי is not stating his own independent view, but rather arguing with the Rabbis, conceding that even according to their view, the document should be returned if the woman is still married.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב פַּפָּא resolves the contradiction by reinterpreting רַבִּי יוֹסֵה's statement. He argues that רַבִּי יוֹסֵה is not contradicting the רַבָּנִים but rather speaking according to their own logic.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב פַּפָּא suggests רַבִּי יוֹסֵה is arguing לְדִידֵיהּ דְּרַבָּנִים. רַבִּי יוֹסֵה concedes that repayment is a concern, but argues that during marriage, repayment is unlikely because the document is not yet subject to payment. This reframes the dispute as an internal refinement rather than a contradiction.

keyTerms.meaning: According to the רַבָּנִים's own view
It is not yet subject to repayment

steps.whatToRemember: רַבִּי יוֹסֵה's ruling is a concession within the רַבָּנִים's framework, not an independent contradiction.
steps.confusionAlert: This resolution changes the nature of רַבִּי יוֹסֵה's statement from an independent ruling to a concession.
steps.whyThisMatters: This allows the Gemara to maintain the original order of the Tannaim without contradiction.

phrases.english: Rav Pappa said:
Actually, do not reverse [the opinions],

phrases.aramaic: רַבִּי יוֹסֵי לְדִבְרֵיהֶם דְּרַבָּנַן קָאָמַר לְהוּ.
phrases.english: Rabbi Yosei was saying to them according to their words of the Rabbis.

phrases.aramaic: לְדִידִי אֲפִילּוּ נִתְאַרְמְלָה אוֹ נִתְגָּרְשָׁה נָמֵי
phrases.english: According to me, even if she was widowed or divorced also
we do not suspect payment.

phrases.aramaic: לְדִידְכוּ אוֹדוֹ לִי מִיהַת בְּעוֹדָהּ תַּחַת בַּעֲלָהּ,
phrases.english: According to you, concede to me at least while she is still under her husband,

phrases.aramaic: דְּיַחֲזִיר לָאִשָּׁה, דְּלָאו בַּת פֵּירָעוֹן הִיא.
phrases.english: that he should return to the woman, that she is not subject to payment.

steps.triggerLanguage: רב פפא אמר: לעולם לא תיפוך, רבי יוסי לדבריהם דרבנן קאמר להו

steps.title: The רַבָּנִים's Rebuttal: Suspicion of Bundles of Money
steps.stepSummary: The Rabbis respond to רבי יוסי's concession by arguing that even during marriage, the husband might have given her bundles of money (צררי) in exchange for the document, so repayment is still a concern.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara presents the רַבָּנִים's counter-argument to רַבִּי יוֹסֵה's point. They maintain that repayment is still a concern even during marriage.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The רַבָּנִים respond: אִימוּר צַרְרֵי אֲתַפְּסָה. Even if the woman is still married, the husband might have given her bundles of money to pay off the כְּתוּבָּה, and she gave him back the document. Therefore, repayment is still a concern, and the document should not be returned.
steps.whatToRemember: The רַבָּנִים maintain that repayment is possible even during marriage.
steps.confusionAlert: This shows the רַבָּנִים's strict stance on preventing double collection.
steps.whyThisMatters: This completes the exchange between רַבִּי יוֹסֵה and the רַבָּנִים.

phrases.aramaic: וַאֲמַרוּ לֵיהּ רַבָּנַן: אֵימוֹר צְרָרֵי אַתְפְּסָה.
phrases.english: And the Rabbis said to him: Say, she seized bundles.

steps.triggerLanguage: ואמרו ליה רבנן: אימור צררי אתפסה

rashi.hebrew: צררי אתפסה - מעות צרורות או כספים מסר לה ליחוד כתובתה כשנשאה או לאחר מכאן שלא יטריחוה יורשים אם תתאלמן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that צררי refers to bundles of coins or silver that the husband gave her to pay off her כתובה, either at the time of marriage or later, so that her heirs would not burden him if she were to become widowed.

steps.title: רַבִּינָא's Resolution: Suspicion of Two Marriage Contracts
steps.stepSummary: Ravina offers a final resolution: the Rabbis suspect that the husband wrote two marriage contracts (שתיהן כתובות), and רבי יוסי does not share this suspicion.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבִּינָא provides an alternative resolution to the contradiction between the two baraitot. He suggests reversing the Tannaim in the first baraita.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּינָא suggests reversing the Tannaim in the first baraita (לְעוֹלָם אִיפּוּךְ קַמַּיִיתָא). The רַבָּנִים in the second baraita suspect that the husband wrote two marriage contracts. If the found document is returned, the woman could collect twice. רַבִּי יוֹסֵה does not share this suspicion.
steps.whatToRemember: The רַבָּנִים suspect the existence of a second marriage contract.
steps.confusionAlert: This resolution introduces a new factor: the possibility of duplicate documents.
steps.whyThisMatters: This provides a final, comprehensive resolution to the sugya's contradictions.

phrases.english: Ravina said: Always reverse the first one.

phrases.aramaic: וְטַעְמָא דְּרַבָּנַן הָכָא, מִשּׁוּם דְּחָיְישִׁינַן לִשְׁתֵּי כְתוּבּוֹת.
phrases.english: And the reason of the Rabbis here, because we are concerned about two marriage contracts.

phrases.aramaic: וְרַבִּי יוֹסֵי לִשְׁתֵּי כְתוּבּוֹת לָא חָיֵישׁ.
phrases.english: And Rabbi Yosei about two marriage contracts is not concerned.

steps.triggerLanguage: רבina אמר: לעולם איפוך קמאייתא. וטעמא דרבנן הכא, משום דחיישינן לשתיהן כתובות

rashi.hebrew: לעולם איפוך קמייתא - ודקשיא לך דרבנן אדרבנן לא תיקשי דטעמייהו דרבנן בכתובה לאו משום דחיישינן לפירעון דמאן דפרע ואהדר ליה שטרא לאלתר קרע ליה אלא משום דחיישינן לשתי כתובות שמא חזר וכתב לה כתובה אחרת שניה לאחר שנפלה זאת מידה ואין הבעל מודה דקתני לאו באומר פרעתי אלא באומר אל תחזרו לה שיש בידה אחרת ותוציא על יורשי שטר אחר שטר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the רבנן's concern in the first baraita is not about payment (פירעון) because a paid document would be torn up, but rather about the possibility of two marriage contracts (שתי כתובות), where the husband wrote a second one after the first was lost and now denies it to prevent the wife from collecting twice from his heirs.

tosafot.hebrew: דחיישינן לשתי כתובות - ובלאו איפוך לא מצי לאוקמא דרבי יוסי חייש לשתי כתובות דא"כ עודה תחת בעלה אמאי יחזיר לאשה כשאין הבעל מודה:
tosafot.english: For we are concerned [that there may be] two kesubah [documents]. The Gemara raised a contradiction between two Baraisos: Baraisa a) on (7a), where the Rabanan say that when one finds a notarized loan document it is not to be returned to the lender, because we consider the possibility that it already had been paid as the husband claims. R’ Yose maintains that we are not concerned about payment, since the document is intact. Had the loan been paid, the document would have been destroyed. Baraisa b) on (7b) where it is evident that when the marriage has already been terminated by divorce or death of the husband, R’ Yose does consider the possibility that a lost kesubah was already paid. The Gemara first reconciled the two Baraisos by saying that the opinions in the first Baraisa are to be switched. It is R’ Yose who is concerned that the loan was paid and the Rabanan who are not concerned that the loan was paid. The Gemara then asked that this would create a problem with the opinion of the Rabanan. In Baraisa a) they say we are not concerned about the possibility that the loan document was paid and in Baraisa b) they maintain that we do not return a lost kesubah to the wife. Ravina now offers a solution to this problem. The two Baraisos are speaking of two different concerns. In Baraisa a) insofar as being concerned that a lost document has already been paid, the Rabanan rule that we need not be concerned about payment, since the document is intact. However, in Baraisa b) where we are discussing a lost kesubah document, we are concerned that a husband who claims I wrote a second substitute kesubah document might be telling the truth. We can therefore not allow the lost kesubah to be returned to his wife. Ravina introduced his resolution by saying that we must switch the opinions of the Baraisa a) as initially suggested by the Gemara. Tosfos questions why this is necessary. Perhaps we can resolve the contradiction between the two Baraisos as follows: Baraisa a) need not be switched and it is R’ Yose who rules in Baraisa a) that we are not concerned that the loan was paid and in Baraisa b) he maintains that we are not concerned the kesubah was paid, but we are concerned that there may be a second kesubah? Without switching the two opinions in Baraisa a) we cannot resolve the contradiction by saying that in Baraisa a) R’ Yose is not concerned that a loan document was paid but in Baraisa b) R’ Yose considers the husband’s claim that he wrote two kesubos, the second as a substitute for the lost first kesubah. For if so, that in Baraisa b) R’ Yose is expressing his concern that the husband truly wrote a substitute kesubah, when she is under the jurisdiction of her husband, i.e. she is still married to him, why would R’ Yose rule that the lost kesubah should be returned to the wife when the husband does not admit that it belongs to her? His claim that he wrote a second kesubah is just as credible when she is still married as it is when the marriage has ended. Insofar as a claim that I paid the kesubah is concerned, there is a huge difference between when the couple is still married, where there never was a need to pay the kesubah and when the marriage ended where the the kesubah should have been paid. However, in regard to the claim “I wrote a second substitute kesubah” there is no difference whether they are still married or divorced. In either case it is possible that she lost her first kesubah and her husband wrote a second substitute kesubah. According to the switched Baraisa a) R’ Yose maintains that he is concerned about payment. In Baraisa b) he is saying that during the marriage there is no concern that the document was paid. The Rabanan in Baraisa a) are never concerned about payment, since the document is intact. In Baraisa b) they say that they are concerned about the possibility of a second substitute kesubah.
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות clarifies that Ravina's resolution of reversing the Tannaim is necessary because without it, רבי יוסי would be concerned about two marriage contracts (שתי כתובות), which would contradict his ruling to return the document when the husband does not admit to writing a second one.

meforshim.interplaySummary: רש״י focuses on explaining the specific nature of the רבנן's concern (שתי כתובות vs. פירעון), while תוספות emphasizes the logical necessity of Ravina's reversal to avoid a contradiction in רבי יוסי's position.

steps.title: רַבִּי אֱלִיעֶזֶר and רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן on טוֹפֶס and תּוֹרֶף
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara introduces the dispute between רבי אלעזר and רבי יוחנן regarding how a document is divided when one holds the טופס and the other holds the תורף.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara presents the foundational statements of רַבִּי אֱלִיעֶזֶר and רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן regarding the division of a document held by two parties. This sets up the central conflict of the sugya.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּי אֱלִיעֶזֶר holds that if one holds the טוֹפֶס and the other the תּוֹרֶף, each takes the part they hold. רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן holds they always divide equally, regardless of which part each holds. This establishes the core dispute over how to allocate rights when physical possession is split.

keyTerms.meaning: The standard or non-essential part of a document.
The essential part of a document containing core details.

steps.whatToRemember: רַבִּי אֱלִיעֶזֶר: each takes what they hold. רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן: always divide equally.
steps.confusionAlert: The terms טוֹפֶס and תּוֹרֶף refer to different parts of a document, with the תּוֹרֶף being the essential part containing the core details.
steps.whyThisMatters: This dispute determines how property rights are allocated when physical possession is split between two parties.

phrases.english: Rabbi Elazar says:

phrases.aramaic: מַחֲלוֹקֶת בְּשֶׁשְּׁנֵיהֶם אֲדוּקִים בַּטּוֹפֶס, וּשְׁנֵיהֶם בַּתּוֹרֶף.
phrases.english: The dispute is when both are grasping the standard part, and both are grasping the…

phrases.aramaic: אֲבָל אֶחָד אָדוּק בַּטּוֹפֶס וְאֶחָד אָדוּק בַּתּוֹרֶף –
phrases.english: But if one is grasping the standard part and one is grasping the essential part –
This one takes the standard part,
and this one takes the essential part.
And Rabbi Yoḥanan says: In all cases they divide.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רַבִּי אֶלְעָזָר: מַחֲלוֹקֶת בְּשֶׁשְּׁנֵיהֶם אֲדוּקִים בַּטּוֹפֶס, וּשְׁנֵיהֶם בַּתּוֹרֶף. אֲבָל אֶחָד אָדוּק בַּטּוֹפֶס וְאֶחָד אָדוּק בַּתּוֹרֶף – זֶה נוֹטֵל טוֹפֶס, וְזֶה נוֹטֵל תּוֹרֶף. וְרַבִּי יוֹחָנָן אָמַר: לְעוֹלָם חוֹלְקִין.

rashi.hebrew: מחלוקת - האי יחלוקו דקאמר ר"ש [ב"ג] דמשמע חולקין בשוה כך שמעתי משום ר' יצחק ב"ר מנחם ונראה בעיני:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבי אלעזר's use of מחלוקת refers to the ruling of רבי שמעון בן גמליאל that the parties should חולקין בשוה, as heard from רבי יצחק בן רבי מנחם.

rashi.hebrew: ושניהם אדוקים בתורף - כלומר או שניהם אדוקין בתורף ותורף גילוי של שטר מקום הלוה והמלוה והמעות והזמן טופס שאר כל לשון השטר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י defines תורף as the essential details of the document (names of parties, amount, date) and טופס as the remaining text, clarifying the scenario where both parties hold the תורף.

rashi.hebrew: זה נוטל כו' - דמאי דתפיס דידיה הוא כדקתני לעיל זה נוטל עד מקום שידו מגעת כו' ולקמן מפרש למאי מיבעי ליה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that each party takes what they hold based on the principle that what one has seized is considered theirs, as stated earlier in the Mishnah.

tosafot.hebrew: מחלוקת - פרש"י דהיינו יחלוקו דקאמר רשב"ג והדין עמו שלא רצה לפרש פלוגתא דרבי ורשב"ג משום דהיכי קאמר אבל אחד אדוק בטופס כו' משמע דהתם לא פליגי וזה אינו דגם שם אם אינו מקוים לא שקיל לרבי מידי:
tosafot.english: The division. This word is the introductory word of R’ Elazar’s statement. What does it mean in the context of his statement? The word divide - has many different meanings. Among the definitions offered by the World Web dictionary we find the following: a) A serious disagreement between two groups of people. b) Separate into parts or portions. The word מחלוקת used in our Gemara can also be translated these two ways. Most often this construction of the word refers to a) a disagreement. Due to the context which it is used in our Gemara, Rashi, (ד'ה מחלוקת) in the name of R’ Yitzchak ben Menachem, tells us that the intended meaning is b) separate into parts or portions. Obviously when R’ Elazar says the מחלוקת is when both are equally in possession of the document, we must be able to explain the end of his statement “but when they are not equally in possession”, in a logical manner. Tosfos agrees with Rashi and also explains why Rashi translated it as he did: In regard to the word מחלוקת in R’ Elazar’s statement, Rashi explained that this refers to Raban Shimon ben Gamliel’s ruling b) the lender and borrower are to divide the debt equally. The continuation of R’ Elazar’s statement is perfectly logical. He is saying that when one litigant is holding the form1See Artscroll (7B3, note 25). and the other the essence, there will not be an equal division because the essence is more valuable than the form as the Gemara will soon explain. Tosfos shows that if we translate מחלוקת as a) a disagreement, the conclusion of R’ Elazar’s statement does not make sense. He is correct, for he did not want to translate the word מחלוקת as referring to the disagreement between Rebbe and Raban Shimon ben Gamliel and R’ Elazar would be saying that their disagreement is only when both are holding the essence of the document or both are holding the form of the document, but they do not disagree when one is holding the essence and the other the form. Because how could he then say: but when one is holding the form etc. [and the other is holding the essence], which implies that they do not argue when one is holding the essence and the other the form. But this is not so. For there as well, when one is holding the essence and the other the form, if [the document] is not notarized, according to Rebbe, [the lender] will not receive anything. Rebbe’s concern that the document cannot be used when it is not notarized does not change when one of the litigants has an advantage over the other. Ultimately, one cannot collect anything with any portion of a non-notarized document because we are concerned that it may be a forgery. Thus, if we translate מחלוקת as a) disagreement, the conclusion of R’ Elazar’s statement is illogical. We must therefore explain מחלוקת as b) a separation of parts and the conclusion of R’ Elazar’s statement makes sense. He is saying that when each of the litigants holds a different part of the document, it is not divided equally, but according to the value of each part as the Gemara will soon explain.
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות agrees with רש״י that מחלוקת refers to the division ruling of רבי שמעון בן גמליאל rather than a disagreement between sages, because interpreting it as a disagreement would make רבי אלעזר's subsequent statement about one holding טופס and the other תורף logically incoherent.

steps.title: Challenge to רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן from a בְּרִיתָא
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges רבי יוחנן's view that they always divide equally, citing a ברייתא that says each takes up to where their hand reaches.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara raises a difficulty against רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's statement using a בְּרִיתָא that supports taking what one holds. This creates a direct conflict that needs resolution.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The בְּרִיתָא states that each person takes the part they are holding, which contradicts רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's view that they always divide equally. This forces the Gemara to clarify the scope of רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling.

keyTerms.meaning: A tannaitic source outside the Mishnah.

keyTerms.term: זֶה נוֹטֵל עַד מָקוֹם שֶׁיָּדוֹ מַגֶּעֶת
keyTerms.meaning: Each takes up to where their hand reaches.

steps.whatToRemember: The בְּרִיתָא supports taking what one holds, challenging רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's equal division rule.
steps.confusionAlert: The בְּרִיתָא is about a garment, but the principle is applied to the document dispute.
steps.whyThisMatters: This challenge forces the Gemara to clarify the scope of רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling.

phrases.aramaic: וַאֲפִילּוּ אֶחָד אָדוּק בַּטּוֹפֶס וְאֶחָד בַּתּוֹרֶף.
phrases.english: And even if one is grasping the essential part and one the standard part.

phrases.aramaic: וְהָתַנְיָא: זֶה נוֹטֵל עַד מָקוֹם שֶׁיָּדוֹ מַגַּעַת!
phrases.english: And it is taught: This one takes up to where his hand reaches!

phrases.aramaic: לָא צְרִיכָא דְּקָאֵי תּוֹרֶף בֵּי מִצְעֵי.
phrases.english: No, it is necessary where the essential part is in the middle.

steps.triggerLanguage: וַאֲפִילּוּ אֶחָד אָדוּק בַּטּוֹפֶס וְאֶחָד בַּתּוֹרֶף. וְהָתַנְיָא: זֶה נוֹטֵל עַד מָקוֹם שֶׁיָּדוֹ מַגַּעַת!

rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's phrase 'ואפילו אחד אדוק' is a rhetorical question challenging רבי יוחנן's ruling.

rashi.hebrew: והא תניא עד מקום שידו מגעת - שלו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the ברייתא states each person takes the portion their hand reaches, which contradicts רבי יוחנן's equal division rule.

rashi.hebrew: דקאי תורף בי מצעי - ומר אמר חדא ומר אמר חדא ולא פליגי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י resolves the contradiction by explaining that the case involves the torn part lying in the middle, allowing both opinions to stand without conflict.

steps.title: Question: What is the Novelty of רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's Ruling?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks what the purpose of רבי יוחנן's statement is if it only applies to a case where the תורף is in the middle.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara raises a question about the necessity of רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling given the resolution provided. This is a clarifying question, not an objection.
steps.deeperAnalysis: If רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן only rules that they divide equally when the תּוֹרֶף is in the middle, it seems obvious that they should divide it equally in that case. The Gemara seeks to understand the specific teaching of רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן.

keyTerms.meaning: What is the purpose of stating?

steps.whatToRemember: The Gemara questions the novelty of רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a clarifying question, not an objection.
steps.whyThisMatters: This question drives the Gemara to uncover the deeper principle behind רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's statement.

phrases.english: If so, what is there to say?
It is not necessary for a case where it is close to one.

phrases.aramaic: מַהוּ דְּתֵימָא: אָמַר לֵיהּ פְּלוֹג הָכִי,
phrases.english: What is it that you would say: He said to him: Divide it this way,
He is teaching us that he said to him:

phrases.aramaic: מַאי חָזֵית דְּפָלְגַתְּ הָכִי? פְּלוֹג הָכִי.
phrases.english: What did you see that you divided it this way? Divide it this way.

steps.title: רַבִּי אַחָא's Question: Why Does Anyone Need Half a Document?
steps.stepSummary: רבי אחא asks רבינא why either party would need half of a document, as it is of no legal use.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבִּי אַחָא raises a practical question about the utility of dividing a document. This is a practical question about the application of the law.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּי אַחָא questions the practicality of רַבִּי אֱלִיעֶזֶר's ruling that each takes the part they hold. If one holds the טוֹפֶס and the other the תּוֹרֶף, neither has a complete document, making the division seemingly useless.

keyTerms.meaning: His flask (metaphor for a useless object).

steps.whatToRemember: רַבִּי אַחָא questions the practical value of dividing a document.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a practical question about the application of the law.
steps.whyThisMatters: This question leads to a deeper understanding of how division is implemented.

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַב אַחָא מִדִּפְתִּי לְרָבִינָא:
phrases.english: Rav Aḥa of Difti said to Ravina:

phrases.aramaic: לְרַבִּי אֶלְעָזָר דְּאָמַר זֶה נוֹטֵל טוֹפֶס
phrases.english: According to Rabbi Elazar who says this one takes the standard part
and that one takes the essential part, why does he need it?

phrases.aramaic: וְכִי לָצוּר עַל פִּי צְלוֹחִיתוֹ הוּא צָרִיךְ?
phrases.english: Does he need it to cover the opening of his flask?
phrases.notes: Refers to the uselessness of holding half a document.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַב אַחָא מִדִּפְתִּי לְרָבִינָא: לְרַבִּי אֶלְעָזָר דְּאָמַר זֶה נוֹטֵל טוֹפֶס וְזֶה נוֹטֵל תּוֹרֶף, לְמָה לֵיהּ? וְכִי לָצוּר עַל פִּי צְלוֹחִיתוֹ הוּא צָרִיךְ?

steps.title: רַבִּינָא's Answer: Division is by Value, Not Physical Parts
steps.stepSummary: רבינא answers that the division is by monetary value, not by physically splitting the document.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבִּינָא provides a concise answer to רַבִּי אַחָא's question. This answer redefines the nature of the division.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּינָא explains that the division is not physical but monetary. The party holding the more valuable part compensates the other for the difference in value. This principle applies to all cases of division, not just documents.
steps.whatToRemember: Division is by monetary value, not physical parts.
steps.confusionAlert: This answer redefines the nature of the division.
steps.whyThisMatters: This principle applies to all cases of division, not just documents.

phrases.english: He said to him: To the values.

rashi.hebrew: לדמי - מעלין בדמים כמה תורף יפה מן הטופס:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that לדמי means the court evaluates the monetary difference between the superior and inferior portions to determine the compensation owed.

steps.title: Value difference in a שטר
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains how the value of a שטר is determined based on whether it has a date.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא explains רבינא's answer by detailing how a date affects a שטר's value. This is a setup explaining the financial logic behind the ruling.
steps.deeperAnalysis: A dated שטר allows collection from liened property, increasing its worth. An undated one lacks this power. The party holding the dated portion receives compensation for the value difference.

keyTerms.meaning: He gives him the difference between the two values.

steps.whatToRemember: A dated שטר is more valuable because it allows collection from liened property.
steps.whyThisMatters: This illustrates how monetary value is assessed in legal documents.

phrases.aramaic: דְּאָמַר הָכִי: שְׁטָרָא דְּאִית בֵּיהּ זְמַן, כַּמָּה שָׁוֵי?
phrases.english: A document that has a date in it, how much is it worth?
And one that does not have a date in it, how much is it worth?

phrases.aramaic: בִּשְׁטָרָא דְּאִית בֵּיהּ זְמַן – גָּבֵי מִמְּשַׁעְבְּדֵי.
phrases.english: In a document that has a date in it — he collects from liened property.
And the other one — he does not collect from liened property.
He gives him the difference between the two.

steps.triggerLanguage: דְּאָמַר הָכִי: שְׁטָרָא דְּאִית בֵּיהּ זְמַן, כַּמָּה שָׁוֵי? וּדְלֵית בֵּיהּ זְמַן, כַּמָּה שָׁוֵי? בִּשְׁטָרָא דְּאִית בֵּיהּ זְמַן – גָּבֵי מִמְּשַׁעְבְּדֵי. וְאִידַּךְ – לָא גָּבֵי מִמְּשַׁעְבְּדֵי. יָהֵיב לֵיהּ הֵיאַךְ דְּבֵינֵי בֵּינֵי.

rashi.hebrew: שטרא דאית ביה זמן כמה שוה - דתורף עדיף מטופס דאית ביה מקום הזמן דאילו שם המלוה והלוה והמעות כתובין אף בטופס שצריך לחזור מעניינו של שטר בשיטה אחרונה והוא עיקר כדתנן (ב"ב דף קסה:) כתוב בו [מלמעלה מנה ומלמטה מאתים] מלמעלה מאתים ומלמטה מנה הכל הולך אחר התחתון אבל זמן לא מיהדר ואי משום חתימת עדים דהוא תחת הטופס הא אית ליה לרשב"ג מודה בשטר שכתבו אין צריך לקיימו והאי מודה שכתבו הוא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the תורף is more valuable than the טופס because it contains the זמן, which is not repeated in the final line of the טופס like the names and amount are, and also because רבי שמעון בן גמליel holds that a שטר written by the borrower does not require קיום of the witnesses.

tosafot.hebrew: דאית ביה זמן כמה שוה - פרש"י דמעות ולוה ומלוה לא שיימינן דכתיבי נמי בשיטה אחרונה בטופס והוא עיקר דתנן (ב"ב דף קסה:) כתוב [בו] מלמעלה מנה כו' ותימה הא אין למידין משיטה אחרונה ולכך עושין חזרת השטר ומתני' דכתוב למעלה מנה לא מיירי בשיטה אחרונה וגם פי' דבעדים לא שיימינן דרשב"ג אית ליה מודה בשטר שכתבו אין צריך לקיימו ופירושו תימה דאם חשב אותו שאינו תופס עדים כאילו יש לו שטר בלא עדים א"כ לרשב"ג נמי לא א"ש דנהי דסבר דאין צריך לקיימו מ"מ כשאין כלל עדי חתימה פסול ועוד במה עדיף תורף מטופס כך השטר פסול בלא טופס כמו בלא תורף דאם נמחק אפי' שיטה אחת מן השטר הוא פסול אלא ודאי יש לומר דבמה שהשטר נפסל אין שמין דכך שוה כשתופס שיטה אחת מן הטופס כמו תופס לוה ומלוה ומעות ועדים והשטר אינו כשר בלא זה כמו בלא זה אבל זמן שהשטר כשר בלא זמנו שמין כמה שוה ובירושלמי איכא א"ר אלעזר הכל הולך אחר מי שתפוס עדים ופליג אגמ' שלנו דמזכיר זמן וסובר דעדי מסירה כרתי והשטר כשר בלא עדי חתימה ושמין כמה שוה שאין צריך לחזור אחר עדי מסירה ולית ליה ההוא לישנא דג"פ (ב"ב דף קע.) דסבר רשב"ג עדי חתימה כרתי:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות challenges רש״י's reasoning, arguing that one cannot derive value from the final line (שיטה אחרונה) and that רבי שמעון בן גמליel still requires witnesses for a valid שטר, concluding instead that we only appraise the value of elements whose absence invalidates the שטר, such as the זמן, whereas the טופס elements are essential for validity and thus not appraised separately.

meforshim.interplaySummary: תוספות disputes רש״י's rationale for why the תורף is more valuable, rejecting the reliance on the final line and the leniency of רבי שמעון בן גמליel regarding witnesses, and instead proposes that valuation applies only to non-essential elements like the זמן, while essential elements like the טופס are not appraised because their absence invalidates the document entirely.

steps.title: Challenge: Division by value vs. physical division
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the principle of division by value by citing cases where physical division seems to be the norm.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a difficulty against the principle of division by value using examples from other cases. This is a transition line introducing a challenge.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא asks if division by value applies universally, even when physical division is mentioned. If physical division is required in those cases, it contradicts the principle of division by value.

phrases.english: "And they divide" — also we said regarding monetary value.
For if you do not say so,

phrases.aramaic: שְׁנַיִם אוֹחֲזִין בְּטַלִּית הָכִי נָמֵי דְּפָלְגִי?
phrases.english: Two holding a tallit — also they divide?
They ruined it! This is not difficult,

steps.triggerLanguage: ״וְיַחְלוֹקוּ״ נָמֵי דַּאֲמַרַן לְדָמֵי. דְּאִי לָא תֵּימָא הָכִי, שְׁנַיִם אוֹחֲזִין בְּטַלִּית הָכִי נָמֵי דְּפָלְגִי? הָא אַפְסְדוּהּ!

tosafot.hebrew: ויחלוקו נמי לדמי - ואין חולקין את החוב כמו שכתוב בשטר אלא כמו ששוה לו למכור מזה יפרע החצי דאי לא תימא הכי אלא חצי החוב ממש גבי טלית מי חולקין ממש הא אפסדוה ולא גרסי' לא דפסקינן לשטרא דא"כ מה מביא מטלית אדרבה בטלית אם יחתכנו לשנים אין פסידא כל כך ואם יחתוך השטר לא ישוה לכלום ועוד דפשיטא הוא דלא יחלוקו השטר לשנים דאוקימנא לעיל פלוגתא דרבי ורבי שמעון בן גמליאל במודה בשטר שכתבו אלא כדפרישית:
tosafot.english: [When Raban Shimon ben Gamliel ruled they should] divide [the document he] also [meant] the value [of the document]. See Artscroll (7b4) who explains that the Gemara is telling us that we should not think that Raban Shimon ben Gamliel is ruling that we tear the document in two, which would of course mean that the lender loses all. Rather, he means that we divide according to the value of the document. Perhaps their explanation is based on Rishonim who do follow that approach. Tosfos rejects this explanation of the Gemara. For the sake of clarity we will label each explanation of the Gemara: 1) The Gemara is rejecting the idea that the document is to be physically split in half and concluding that it is divided according to its value. 2) The Gemara is rejecting the idea that we divide the face value of the document between creditor and debtor and concluding that we divide the market value of said document between creditor and debtor. Tosfos first states that explanation 2) is correct. He will then explain why he rejects explanation 1). The Gemara is saying: We do not divide the face value of the document, rather we divide the market value of the document, which is always less than the face value. From this market value [the debtor] pays half. For example: When the face value of the document is one thousand dollars, the market value of the document may be only nine hundred dollar. The debtor must pay the creditor only four hundred fifty dollars. Tosfos shows us how his understanding works with the continuation of the Gemara: For if you do not say that we divide the market value, but rather half of the actual debt, which is the face value, in regard to a cloak where our Mishna also ruled it is to be divided, do we actually divide it, that would certainly damage it. We see that the term “they divide” does not mean a physical division, rather a division of value. So too, the division of a loan document does not mean that we divide the face value of the loan, but the market value. There were manuscripts of the Gemara that differed from our text. Their text read: ויחלוקו נמי לא דפסקינן לשטרא - when we say “they divide” it does not mean that they split the document.1See ישא ברכה. According to this text it is clear that the Gemara is working with explanation 1) and is rejecting physically splitting the document. Tosfos must explain why that text is incorrect. We do not have the text - not that we split the document, which clearly says that the initial explanation was that the document is split. Tosfos offers two reasons for rejecting that text and explanation 1). The first: for if so, why is [the Gemara] citing the case of dividing a cloak to prove that division means the value and not the actual cloak? The flow of the Gemara’s proof must be: Even if you insist that “they divide” in Raban Shimon ben Gamliel’s statement means physical division, that explanation is definitely untenable when discussing the cloak mentioned in our Mishna. On the contrary, if a cloak is split in two the damage will not be so great, whereas if a document is split it is worthless.2See ריטב"א for a defense of the earlier text and explanation 1). If we might accept the explanation that a document can be physically split in two, we certainly could accept that a cloak is physically split in two. There is absolutely no logical proof from the case of a cloak to the case of a document. However, if the choice is between dividing the face value versus the market value, we can say that the Gemara is proving that just as in the case of the cloak, “they divide” does not mean a physical division, because that would entail a loss, but rather “they divide” refers to market value, so too, when discussing a document the term “they divide” means the market value and not the face value. The second reason for rejecting explanation 1): Furthermore, it is plain that the Gemara knows that the document is not to be physically divided in two, because, earlier (7a) we explained that the disagreement between Rebbe and Raban Shimon ben Gamliel is about whether when a borrower] admits that the document was legally written, does the lender have to notarize the signatures or not in order to collect. This dispute makes sense only if the document is going to be used for collection. If it is going to be torn in two, there is certainly no need to certify the signatures. If so, there was no need for the Gemara to prove that the document is not torn in two. However, if we say that the Gemara is proving that we divide the market value as opposed to the face value, there most certainly a need for notarization since the borrower will be collecting some money with this document. Rather, the truth is as we have explained. Explanation 2) is correct. The Gemara is rejecting a division of the face value in favor of a division of market value.
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the Gemara's challenge regarding division by value (לדמי) is not about physically tearing the document, but rather clarifies that we do not divide the face value of the debt, but rather divide the market value of the document, from which the debtor pays half.
